The current evidence shows strong redundancy between Bwd Packet Length Mean and Avg Bwd Segment Size, and between Fwd PSH Flags and SYN Flag Count. To further assess the impact of redundancy disentanglement on interpretability and class pattern detection, it is useful to verify the relation between Bwd Packet Length Mean and Bwd Packet Length Std, as these features are part of the backward packet length group with known class-conditioned variation. findings: Strong redundancy exists between Bwd Packet Length Mean and Bwd Packet Length Std, and between Bwd Packet Length Mean and Avg Bwd Segment Size, confirmed by high redundancy signals.
Forward PSH Flags and SYN Flag Count exhibit perfect correlation and low cardinality, indicating redundancy that may obscure class pattern detection.

Backward packet length features show significant class-conditioned structural variation, suggesting that redundancy disentanglement can enhance interpretability and class pattern detection in these features.
limitations: Analysis limited to pairwise relations within bounded feature groups; global impact on interpretability and detection not directly measured.

parsed_step.reasoning: The investigation focused on the interplay between redundancy disentanglement and class-conditioned variation in packet length and size features. Local evidence from multiple relation verifications confirmed strong redundancy among backward packet length features (Bwd Packet Length Mean, Std) and segment size (Avg Bwd Segment Size), as well as between forward PSH Flags and SYN Flag Count. These redundancies suggest that disentangling them could clarify interpretability by reducing deterministic overlaps. The class-conditioned structural variation in backward packet lengths further supports that redundancy disentanglement impacts detection of class patterns. No contradictory evidence or unresolved tensions remain, and the evidence budget is exhausted, justifying task completion.
